docs.text: The Aether: Spatial Behavior Software Suite, is a set of tools and interfaces for designing the behavior of active and responsive environments. Aether also provides tools to facilitate the long-term operation of these spaces. Use Cases. ¶ Developed by the Living Architecture Systems Group and Philip Beesley Studio Inc. Aether was originally created to support Living Architecture Testbeds, and its design has been informed by many years of testing and evolution in the control systems of these deeply immersive environments. Key Concepts. ¶ Distributed Topology: ¶ Entities in Aether are designed for distributed computation. This allows the system to be scalable (new devices add minimal computational overhead to the central server), and robust (entities are resilient to network disruption and can continue basic operation without a connection to the server) Spatial Behavior: ¶ Entities in Aether exist in 3D space, and their spatial relationships to their environment and other entities inform their behavior. Thin Shell Interface: ¶ Aether is designed as a Thin Shell , a co-ordinating interface that can speak to many different kinds of hardware and software using open protocols (generally OSC)

docs.text: Home ¶ Introduction ¶ The Aether software suite establishes and maintains the behaviour system for Living Architecture Testbeds. It consists of four components which operate together to enable persistent, coordinated and controllable behaviour within the testbed: Microcontroller firmware (C++) within each ESP-32 Node Controller (NC) device within the system establishes a distributed means for elements to respond to influences A Node.js server (Javascript) to coordinate the distributed elements within the system and serve the browser-based control pages Browser-based software (Javascript) to create Graphical User Interface (GUI) elements for controlling behaviour logic and influences within the environment A realtime database (Firebase) to maintain the configuration of the system, its settings and its various user sessions and contexts Key functional principles used in the testbeds include Influence Engines : Layered, spatialized algorithms that establish influence on elements of the system Reactor Cells : Elements within a node-controller that respond to stimulus via coordinated time-based reaction profiles Subscription : Means by which specific node controllers become aware of and sensitive to influences Because the system is distributed among various platforms, code is reused wherever possible. To enable functional aspects of the browser-based GUI representations of node controllers, for example, some of the C++ code that the physical microcontrollers use is compiled into Web Assembly (WASM) modules that are integrated into the javascript running on these pages. Similarly, the core of each Influence Engine is implemented once in C++ and then used as a WASM within the browser to visualize the effect of the influence engines. Guides: ¶ Guides provide step by step walkthroughs for: Setting up a new testbed Creating a new Influence Engine

docs.text: Setting up the Build System ¶ To modify Firmware for a node controller you will need Zig installed. To manage Zig version we will install ZVM. Run curl https://www.zvm.app/install.sh | bash Run source /home/aether/.bashrc to start using ZVM in the same terminal Run zvm install 0.15.1 to install the specific version of zig we need. Zig should now be installed. To test that firmware compilation is working switch to the firmware directory with cd NCv4_Firmware and run zig build (this will take several minutes). This command will recompile the Web Assembly Modules used for the Influence Engines and Logic Editor. To get the compilation system ready to build firmware for our Node Controller Devices, we also need to install PlatformIO. run the included python script to install Platform IO, python3 get-platformio.py add pio to the PATH. run sudo nano ~/.bashrc scroll to the bottom and add the line export PATH="$PATH:$HOME/.platformio/penv/bin" use CTRL X to exit, Y to confirm changes and ENTER to complete the addition run source ~/.bashrc for these changes to take effect in this terminal Platform IO should now be installed, you can test its working by running zig build arduino for compile firmware for Node Controller devices You are now setup to edit Aether's Influence Engines and Logic Reactor Blocks and compile them to Web Assembly for the user interface, and to device firmware.

Creating a Logic Reactor Block. ¶ Creating a custom logic editor block allows you to add new functionality to logic graphs made with the logic editor. In this example, we'll create a block that implements both the OR and Exclusive OR logical operators. Creating a new logic block will involve editing the following files Adding the new block class in NCv4_Firmware\Blocks.h Adding our block to the block constructor in NCv4_Firmware\Blocks.cpp Creating a new module in Testbed_Server\public\logic\modules\[NEW BLOCK].js Updating the list of modules in Testbed_Server\public\logic\modules.js Adding a New Block to Blocks.h ¶ Block Class Structure ¶ We'll start by creating the functionality of the block in NCv4_Firmware\Blocks.h . Each block is defined as a class within this file, and each new block inherits from the parent Block class. A typical block class is structured like this: class [BlockName] : public Block { public: // declare inputs and their types float value = 0.0; // declare properties and their types. float property = 0.0; // declare outputs and their types. float outputA = 0.0; float outputB = 0.0; // Initialize our inputs and outputs virtual void init() { inputs.addInput("value", &value, {}, 0); inputs.addProperty("property", &property, {.min = 0, .max = 1, .step = 0.1}); outputs.add("outputA", &outputA, {}); outputs.add("outputB", &outputB, {}); } // define the logic that happens when our block runs virtual void execute(unsigned long millis) { //logic goes here } }; Variable Declaration ¶ For our OR block we'll be looking to create a block with 2 boolean inputs, a parameter that allows the user to set if this is an OR or an Exclusive OR, and a single output. We'll set up the block initially with the following: class OrBlock : public Block { public: // declare inputs and their types bool inputA = false; bool inputB = false; // declare properties and their types. bool exclusive = false; // declare outputs and their types. bool output = false; Initialization ¶ Once we've declared our inputs, parameters, and outputs, we need to initialize our block. Initializing adds our blocks and parameters to the Input and Output tables ( Tables are a internal data structure used throughout Aether to facilitate memory access and management within WASM's ). When we add parameters to the Table we can define some extra options that will determine how the User Interface is will be generated. Adding an input uses the addInput() method of the inputs table, which looks like: inputs.addInput("inputA", &inputA, {}, false); where: "inputA" is the String Name of our input &inputA is the memory address of our inputA variable declared above ( the & operator returns the memory address of a variable in c++ ) { } is the set of additional options declared for this input, for simple inputs this can typically be left empty false is the declared baseline for this input (what the input defaults to if nothing is connected) Adding a property uses the addProperty() of the inputs table which looks like: inputs.addProperty("property", &property, {.min = 0, .max = 1, .step = 0.1}); This is very similar to the addInput method, but Properties do not have a baseline value defined, and often make use of the additional options argument. Available options that can be defined for a property are: Type Property Description Default Value float .min The Minimum value for this property 0 float .max The Maximum value for this property 1.0 float .step The Step size to use for the UI slider ( smaller values give finer control ) 0.01 bool .display Should this property be displayed in the UI true char .unit Units to display for this value (mm, cm, m/s etc.) None Adding an output uses the add() method of the outputs table, which looks like: outputs.add("outputA", &outputA, {}); where: "outputA" is the String Name of our input &outputA is the memory address of our inputA variable declared above ( the & operator returns the memory address of a variable in c++ ) { } is the set of additional options declared for this input, for simple inputs this can typically be left empty Using these Methods we can construct the init() method of our new block. // Initialize our inputs and outputs virtual void init() { inputs.addInput("inputA", &inputA, {}, false); inputs.addInput("inputB", &inputB, {}, false); // We won't use any additional options here as our property is a boolean value inputs.addProperty("exclusive", &exclusive, {}); outputs.add("output", &output, {}); } Executing Logic ¶ With our inputs, properties and outputs initialized we can start writing the logic of our block. The execute function does not return a value, it sets the output variables declared above to the desired value based on the inputs and parameters. ( Note the execute function always receives a relative time in milliseconds as an argument, which can be useful for blocks dealing with time, however it is not used in this example ) The execute function for our or block looks like this: virtual void execute(unsigned long millis) { if (exclusive){ output = (inputA || inputB) && (inputA != inputB); } else{ output = inputA || inputB; } } The full class of our OrBlock block to be added to the blocks.h file looks like: class OrBlock : public Block { public: // declare inputs and their types bool inputA = false; bool inputB = false; // declare properties and their types. bool exclusive = false; // declare outputs and their types. bool output = false; // Initialize our inputs and outputs virtual void init() { inputs.addInput("inputA", &inputA, {}, false); inputs.addInput("inputB", &inputB, {}, false); // We won't use any additional options here as our property is a boolean value inputs.addProperty("exclusive", &exclusive, {}); outputs.add("output", &output, {}); } // Execute our logic virtual void execute(unsigned long millis) { if (exclusive){ output = (inputA || inputB) && (inputA != inputB); } else{ output = inputA || inputB; } } }; Updating the Block Constructor in Blocks.cpp ¶ Now that we've created our block class, we need to add it to the Block constructor defined in NCv4Firmware\Blocks.cpp . The block constructor starts around line 13 and begins with Block *newBlock(const char *type) { followed by a long list of conditional statements. To the end of this list of conditionals, we need to add the condition of our new block. We'll do this by adding another else if condition at the end of the list before the errorLog(" Can't find %s in Blocks.cpp .... ", type); warning. Our new condition will look like: else if (strcmp(type, "OrBlock") == 0){ return new OrBlock(); } This completes the creation of the block on the firmware side of the Aether Code base. Recompile WASM's and Firmware. ¶ With the firmware side complete we can rebuild the Web Assembly Modules from the NCv4_Firmware folder by running zig build in the terminal. We can also upload firmware with our new block to our devices via OTA or directly over usb. In the next section we'll create the files necessary to include our new block in the Logic Editor UI. Creating a new module ¶ To make our new block display in the User Interface of the Logic Reactor we need to create a new module that defines how the block will be drawn. This module will be added to Testbed_Server\public\logic\modules\ and should match the class name of the block defined in Blocks.h . for this example we'll create OrBlock.js and add it to the modules folder. The following template can be used to create a new block module: import { html } from "../../global.js" import { Position } from "../../libraries/xyflow.js" import { NodeContainer } from "../components.js" import { useErrorSignal } from "../../components/hooks.js"; import { millis } from "../shared.js" export const inputs = { } export const outputs = { } export function initWasm(data) { } export function execute({}, data) { data.wasm.executeBlock(millis()); } export function Display({data}) { return html` <${NodeContainer} data=${data}>
` } export function DisplayIcon() { return html` <${NodeContainer}>
` } The inputs and outputs constants need to be populated with the inputs and outputs of our block. Each input and output must be added as a Javascript Object, with the following properties: inputA: { type: "number", description: "First value", position: Position.Left, }, Property Description type The Javascript type of this input (*in general this should always be "number") desciription A description of this input position determines where the input will appear on the block. Options are Position.Left, Position.Right, Position.Top, Position.Bottom. Note we generally use Left or Top for inputs, with the outputs directly across from the input (Right or Bottom) Note The spelling of the input or output must match exactly with the String Name of the input or output that was defined in the initialization function of the block class. The complete input and output constants for our OR block would look like: export const inputs = { inputA: { type: "number", description: "First Value", position: Position.Left, }, inputB: { type: "number", description: "Second Value", position: Position.Left, }, } export const outputs = { output: { type: "number", description: "Result", position: Position.Right, }, } The Display() and DisplayIcon() functions allow us to describe how a block will look in the Logic Editor using Javascript and HTML. Replacing the Text in the template with text will create a simple text label for the block. In the Display() function we also have access to data about inputs, outputs and parameters of the block. In our example, we'll use this to change our simple text label depending on the 'exclusive' property of our block: export function Display({data}) { const exclusive_ref = useErrorSignal(data.data.settings, "exclusive") let label_text = "OR" if(exclusive_ref.value){label_text = "XOR"} return html` <${NodeContainer} data=${data}>
${label_text}
` } We use const exclusive_ref = useErrorSignal(data.data.settings, "exclusive") call to get a reference to the "exclusive" property of our block. We can check the value of this reference using exclusive_ref.value and use this to change the text that appears in the block label. The full code of our OrBlock.js file is: import { html } from "../../global.js" import { Position } from "../../libraries/xyflow.js" import { NodeContainer } from "../components.js" import { useErrorSignal } from "../../components/hooks.js"; import { millis } from "../shared.js" export const inputs = { inputA: { type: "number", description: "First Value", position: Position.Left, }, inputB: { type: "number", description: "Second Value", position: Position.Left, }, } export const outputs = { output: { type: "number", description: "Result", position: Position.Right, }, } export function initWasm(data) { } export function execute({}, data) { data.wasm.executeBlock(millis()); } export function Display({data}) { const exclusive_ref = useErrorSignal(data.data.settings, "exclusive") let label_text = "OR" if(exclusive_ref.value){label_text = "XOR"} return html` <${NodeContainer} data=${data}>
${label_text}
` } export function DisplayIcon() { return html` <${NodeContainer}>
OR
` } Updating Modules.js ¶ Finally we need to update the Testbed_Server\public\logic\modules.js file to include our new module. Open the file and add the following to the imports at the top of the file: import * as OrBlock from "./modules/OrBlock.js"; We also need to add our imported OrBlock to the export const modules = { list below. Once this has been added our new Logic Block is complete!

docs.text: Editing Aether ¶ Introduction ¶ For more advanced use, users may want to access and edit Aether's source code, to create new Influence Engines , or new Logic Reactor Blocks . With Raspberry Pi Image ¶ Connect to the Raspberry Pi with VSCode Remote (using the same password as SSH) Open the testbed-v4 folder Open a terminal. Building WASM's and Firmware ¶ Note If you are using the latest Rapsberry Pi Image, your Pi should already have the necessary libraries and tools installed to build Aethers firmware and WASM's. If Zig and PlatformIO are not installed on your system see the guide on setting up the build system Building Web Assembly Modules (WASM's) ¶ Aether uses Web Assembly Modules to share code between the device firmware and web interface. To compile the Web Assembly Modules for the web interface: move to the NCv4_Firmware directory with cd NCv4_Firmware run zig build This will compile the Web Assembly Modules and move them to their correct locations for the web interface to use them. Building Firmware for Devices ¶ Running zig build arduino will compile firmware binaries for all supported device types. Over the Air Firmware Updates ¶ If a device is already connected to Aether, you can use the Over the Air (OTA) update system in the Aether Dashboard. Select the device you'd like to update, and press the "OTA Firmware Update" button in the Hardware -> Troubleshooting Tools panel of the dashboard. This will send the last firmware compiled on the Raspberry Pi to this device. Directly Connected Firmware Update, ¶ For Devices that are not connected to Aether, you can compile firmware directly to the device. Connect the device to the Raspberry Pi using a micro usb cable. Ensure your remote terminal is in the NCv4_Firmware folder run pio run -e featheresp32 --target upload This will compile the firmware and upload it to the device.
docs.title: Editing Aether

docs.text: Note If you are using the latest Rapsberry Pi Image, your Pi should already have the necessary libraries and tools installed to build Aethers firmware and WASM's. If Zig and PlatformIO are not installed on your system see the guide on setting up the build system
docs.title: Building WASM's and Firmware

docs.text: Aether uses Web Assembly Modules to share code between the device firmware and web interface. To compile the Web Assembly Modules for the web interface: move to the NCv4_Firmware directory with cd NCv4_Firmware run zig build This will compile the Web Assembly Modules and move them to their correct locations for the web interface to use them.
docs.title: Building Web Assembly Modules (WASM's)

docs.text: Running zig build arduino will compile firmware binaries for all supported device types.
docs.title: Building Firmware for Devices

docs.text: If a device is already connected to Aether, you can use the Over the Air (OTA) update system in the Aether Dashboard. Select the device you'd like to update, and press the "OTA Firmware Update" button in the Hardware -> Troubleshooting Tools panel of the dashboard. This will send the last firmware compiled on the Raspberry Pi to this device.
docs.title: Over the Air Firmware Updates

docs.text: For Devices that are not connected to Aether, you can compile firmware directly to the device. Connect the device to the Raspberry Pi using a micro usb cable. Ensure your remote terminal is in the NCv4_Firmware folder run pio run -e featheresp32 --target upload This will compile the firmware and upload it to the device.
docs.title: Directly Connected Firmware Update,

docs.text: Creating an Influence Engine ¶ Warning This documentation page is a work in progress and is not complete. Introduction to Influence Engines ¶ Influence Engines are Algorithms At the heart of LASG’s behaviour system is the concept of Influence Engines. An influence engine is a piece of software that algorithmically generates ‘influences’ on the various spatially distributed parts of the sculpture. For example, a simple influence engine might create waveforms that move through the sculpture by determining the amplitude of a wave at various physical locations in the space. A different Influence Engine might use particle dynamics to calculate the trajectory of virtual objects which influence elements within the sculpture. Overview ¶ An influence engine needs a name, for this guide the influence engine will be called Ambient Waves, and it will simply create an infinite moving sine wave of influence, with specific angle, period, amplitude and velocity (seen from plan view). This wave will exert an influence proportional to the amplitude of the sine wave in any given location (offset so that its influence is read from 0.0-1.0). Inputs, outputs, parameters and events Browser-based visualization and graphical interface Third-party tool integration (eg. Max MSP) Events ¶ Firmware ¶ Implement an influence Engine in CPP ¶ Copy ExampleEngine.h to AmbientWaves.h and rename all the instances of ExampleEngine to AmbientWaves. Copy ExampleEngine.cpp to AmbientWaves.cpp and rename all instances of ExampleEngine to AmbientWaves Add the required header files needed, for ambient waves only math.h is needed #include "math.h" Add the ambient waves paremeters to the class definition in AmbientWaves.h : float velocity = 0.1 ; float amplitude = 0.25 ; float period = 0.2 ; float angle = M_PI / 2 ; Register them in populateTable method of AmbientWaves.cpp so they can be sent over OSC and shown in the dashboard tb . add ( "velocity" , & velocity , {. min = 0 , . max = 2 , . step = 0.01 }); tb . add ( "angle" , & angle , {. min = 0 , . max = 2 * M_PI , . step = 0.01 }); tb . add ( "period" , & period , {. min = 0 , . max = 1 , . step = 0.01 }); tb . add ( "amplitude" , & amplitude , {. min = 0 , . max = 1 , . step = 0.01 }); The influence part of the influence engine is calculate in the influenceOn method Register Influence Engine in base LASG code ¶ Compile firmware ¶ Compiling A WASM Influence Engine ¶ Create & compile WASM Influence Engine wrapper To recompile an existing WASM Influence engine. Run zig build in the NCv4_Firmware directory. This will build all IE's in the zig.build config file and save the compiled WASMs to the Testbed_Server/public directory. To add a new IE to the zig build step, add a buildWasm() call to to the pub fn build(b: *std.Build) function. For example, the ambient waves build function looks like: buildWasm(b, "AmbientWaves", &.{ "AmbientWaves.cpp", "AmbientWavesWasm.cpp", }, optimize); Register the Influence Engine’s wasm binary in the dashboard GUI ¶ Specify basic tools and components Implement specific IE visualization [ tbd ]
docs.title: Creating an Influence Engine

docs.text: Advanced Testbed Setup ¶ For complex testbeds, testbed data can be authored in an external 3D modeling software such as Blender, and uploaded to the server via the LASD file format. Testbed Data: Testbed data describes the spatial locations, and metadata that describe the actuators, sensors & node controllers that make up a testbed. This data is provided using the "Living Architecture Systems Description" (LASD) format. Testbed Image: The testbed image is the background image displayed in the Influence Engine dashboard. 1) Authoring Testbed Data: ¶ Testbed Data in the LASD format can be authored in the 3D modeling tools Blender or Rhino and exported using the LASD plugins (install information for LASD plugins can be found here ). To export Testbed Data directly from Blender to an Aether Database configure the following settings in the LASD Database panel: Database URL: The URL of your Aether Server instance Database Name: The name of your Aether Database Database Path: The name of the Testbed you want to export this LASD data too Auth Port: The authorization port for your Aether Database (Default is typically 9099) Database Port: The port for the Aether Database (Default is typically 9000) API Key: The API Key for your Aether Database Username: The username for your account in your Aether Database Password: The password for your account in your Aether Database Once those setting have been configured, use the Export to Database button to export this testbed to the Database. Troubleshooting Export Errors If there are errors with the export, try running the Sanitize Names for Database Export command, this will attempt to replace any invalid characters that cause issues when exporting ( Blender .### suffix on object names is typically the culprit here ) 2) Adding a Testbed Plan Image: ¶ To add a custom background image that will display in the Aether Dashboard: Create a plan view image plan view can be created in the tool of your testbed in the tool of your choice. The image must be saved as a .png. Note When creating a background image, ensure that the image is drawn using the same pixel:meter scale as defined in the testbed Metadata. Add the image to the Aether Server: plan images should be located in the Testbed_Server/public/plan_images folder and be named with the following format [YOUR TESTBED NAME]_plan.png
docs.title: Advanced Testbed Setup

docs.text: Compiling NC Firmware ¶ Setting up the compilation buildchain. ¶ Install Platformio Cli python get-platformio.py in NCv4_Firmware directory Install Zig In the NCv4_Firmware directory run zig build arduino to compile the firmware. The compiled binary can be found at NCv4_Firmware/build/NCv4_Firmware.ino.bin Over The Air Update ¶ In the NCv4_Firmware directory run zig build arduino to create the OTA bins On the Testbed Dashbaord select the node controller you wish to update In the Hardware tab select OTA Update to send the new NC.bin to all selected NC's Warning For major firmware changes test the update on one NC before uploading to all NC's. Issues in major changes can lead to the NC being stuck in a boot loop, and in these cases working firmware will need to be reuploaded over a usb connection. Doing this for a large number of NC's is a hassle. Upload over USB ¶ Attach the NC to your PC via USB in the NCv4_Firmware directory run pio device list to see if the NC is discovered and see it's port run pio run -t upload -e [ENV] where [ENV] is the env from platformio.ino for the plugged in board Adding a board configuration ¶ Use pio boards list [search term] to find the correct board name Add an environment to platformio.ini where board is the name from 1 Add build flags OTA_SUFFIX string for differentiating OTA binaries (required) other c macro defintions can be used to control compilation Add board to build.zig in the array named boards (search for const boards = ) using the format .{<board name>, <OTA_SUFFIX>},

docs.text: list ¶ Description ¶ A growable list that acts similarly to std::vector but only depends on the c standard library not cpp It can be iterated over using a for each loop, and a refrence to items can be accessed using the [] operator API Refrence ¶ Header File ¶ #include "list.h" Definition ¶ template < typename T > struct list ; Member functions ¶ void push ( T item ); Allocates if space needed for item and adds item to the end of the list. T * get ( int index ); Returns a pointer to the item at index . void remove ( int index ); Removes the item at index from the list and moves items down to fill in the empty space. int len (); Returns the amount of items in the list. void free (); Deallocates the internal item storage, if T is dynamically allocated then each item must be freed, if the list struct is dynamically allocated then the caller is responsible for freeing that memory Examples ¶ list < int > numbers ; numbers . push ( 12 ); numbers . push ( 15 ); numbers . push ( 7 ) numbers . remove ( 1 ); // numbers: 12, 7 bool is_true = numbers [ 1 ] == 7 ; bool is_also_true = * numbers . get ( 1 ) == 7 ; for ( int & number : numbers ) { number = number * 2 ; } // numbers: 24, 14 numbers [ 1 ] // 14

docs.text: Coordinate Spaces ¶ When writing draw functions in the Aether Dashboard. It's helpful to understand the 3 co-ordinate spaces used in dashboardP5.js World Space: the "Real World" coordinates of objects 3D Left Handed +Z Up +Y Forward (+Y up in plan view) +X Right coordinate system. Measured in mm. The origin of the World Coordinate space is typically defined by the origin point of the tool that authored the Testbed LASD data (typically the Blender or Rhino world origin). Grid Space: 3D Left Handed +Z Up, +Y Backwards (+Y Down in plan view) +X Right coordinate system. (we don't really use the z much here, but grid space is still 3D) Grid space is measured in pixels, The Testbed background image should be centered exactly in grid space Origin defined by the plan_offset key in the testbed metadata, Pixel Scale (relative to world space) defined by the plan_ratio key in the testbed metadata ( ratio of pixels to mm ) The maximum extents of the grid space is determined by the size of the testbed background image (in px) and our minimum zoom value. Canvas Space: this is our "viewport" into grid space: Canvas Space extents are determined by the actual canvas size on the webpage. Canvas Space origin is offset from grid space by the p5 grid properties xoff and yoff . (Panning) Scaled relative to Grid Space by the zoom value. Conversion Functions ¶ The following conversion functions, located in dashboardP5.js can be used to convert between the Coordinate spaces: worldToGridCoords([x,y,z]) -> {x:x,y:y,z:z} gridToWorldCoords({x:x,y:y,z:z}) -> {x:x,y:y,z:z} canvasToGridCoords({x:x,y:y},z,p5 instance) -> {x:x,y:y,z:z} Also the worldMouse() function on the P5 instance can be called to return the current mouse position in World Space.
docs.title: Coordinates

docs.text: Hooks & Signals ¶ Signals are used to automatically update UI components when the state of the application changes. Signals in Aether's UI make use of Preact's Hooks and Signals , as well as Aether's Observable object to simplify creating UI components that are sync'd with Aether's Database. Each type of Signal references a Observable object and a key which defines the value on the State Proxy object that is being listened to, or changed. When to Use Signals Signals should be used in contexts where the current state of the application needs to be kept in sync with the database, but there is no re-occurring update loop. For contexts with an update loop, like a p5.js canvas, us a get() (or one of the get helpers) instead. When to Use use Each signal described below has a memoized varient use<SingalName> . These memoized hooks can only be used within Preact function components. When creating a signal outside of a Preact component, use the standard version of the signal not the use<SingalName> , version. objectSignal(): ¶ objectSignal takes a Preact Signal, a Observable object and a Key. When the value at the specified Key is changed (say by a UI component using an objectSignal) the signal updates the State Proxy object, which then updates the database. If the Key that the objectSignal is listening to is updated in the database the signal value will be updated as well. The useObjetSignal function takes only the Observable object and Key, and creates a memoized objectSignal. errorSignal(): ¶ errorSignal takes a Preact Signal, a Observable object, and a Key. Like objectSignal, when the value at the specified Key is changed (say by a UI component using an the errorSignal) the signal updates the State Proxy object, which then updates the database. If the Key that the errorSignal is listening to is updated in the database the signal value will be updated as well. an errorSignal also keeps track of whether or not its Observable object was successful in updating the database. If updating the database was unsuccessful, then the error signal reverts the Observable's local value back to the last successfully set value. The useErrorSignal function takes only the Observable object and Key, and creates a memoized errorSignal. dependentSignal(): ¶ dependentSignal takes a dependency (another signal), a "getObj" expression (which returns an Observable object, typically using the logic dependent on the value of the dependency), and a Key. dependentSignal can be used to await loading, and to setup signals for values who's database path changes depending on the current state of the application (Influence Engine parameters in live mode or not, for example). If the dependency is undefined the dependentSignal will be in a loading state and also be undefined until the dependency resolves to a value. After loading, if the value of the dependency changes the dependentSignal recomputes the getObj expression to return a Observable object based on the dependencies new value. The recomputed value of the dependentSignal will then be the Key's corresponding value in the returned Observable object. The useDependentSignal function creates a memoized dependentSignal. Example An example of a dependent signal for the current preset name for cloudGen: The dependentSignal would be written as: const currentPresetName = useDependentSignal(live, () => currentSetting("cloudGen"), "name"); Where: live is another signal connected to value at users.<SESSION_ID>.live () => currentSetting("CloudGen") is the getObj expression. currentSetting() is a helper function which returns the object at the module name provided, in this case, "CloudGen". The logic in the currentSetting() function depends on the value at users.<SESSION_ID>.live to determine the path to the object it returns. If live is false currentSetting("CloudGen") returns the object at <TESTBED>.transient.sessions.<SESSION_ID>.CloudGen . If live is true currentSetting("CloudGen") returns the object at <TESTBED>.<SCENE>.CloudGen name is the key within the setting object we want this signal to listen for changes to. When the value of users.<SESSION_ID>.live changes this dependentSignal is notfied by the live signal, and re-evaluates which Observable object it should listen to the "name" key on, to be either: <TESTBED>.transient.sessions.<SESSION_ID>.CloudGen> if live is false or, <TESTBED>.<SCENE>.CloudGen if live is true. stateSignal Many dependentSignals use stateSignal as their dependency to wait for the database to be loaded. stateSignal is undefined until the entire database is loaded. Once loading is complete the value of stateSignal contains the state of the entire database. stateSignal can be imported with: import {stateSignal} from "../global.js"; liveSignal The live state of the users session is another common dependency. liveSignal , a signal connected to the live state, can be imported to use as a dependency with: import { liveSignal } from '/libraries/utilities.js'
docs.title: Hooks & Signals

docs.text: Observable ¶ API Refrence ¶ observable(init, path) ¶ import { observable } from "/libraries/observable.js" function observable ( init , path ) { } Parameters ¶ Name Type Description init Object Object to add observation too, recursively wraps objects with a Proxy to ovveride the getter and setter path string Optional, if called with this parameter then all values set on this object will be synced to firebase observe(state, observer, key) ¶ import { observe } from "/libraries/observable.js" function observe ( state , observer , key ) {} Parameters ¶ Name Type Description state observable Object to observe changes too observer (state, fromDatabase, key, undoing) => void function that will be called when a property of state changes key string Optional, if provided observer will only be called when the value of this property changes Return Value ¶ Function to remove the observer observeKeys(state, observer) ¶ import { observeKeys } from "/libraries/observable.js" export function observeKeys ( state , observer ) {} Parameters ¶ Name Type Description state observable Object to observe key changes too observer (state, key, fromDatabase, undoing) => void function that will be called when properties are added or removed from state Return Value ¶ Function to remove the observer get(state, path, last) ¶ import { get } from "/libraries/observable.js" export function get ( state , path , last ) {} Parameters ¶ Name Type Description state object path string deep path on state each property is delimetted by . for example thing.colour last any Optional, if provided will be used to set the last property if it is not undefined Old Docs ¶ State proxy is a wrapper around a javascript object. It integrates with firebase, wrapping around a regular javascript object and tracking when things are set and get from the object. It can also include where in firebase each object is, and automatically sync between the object and firebase. To setup up a regular proxy for subscribing to value changes or for history use you can just call proxy(objName) to make a proxy that wraps a firebase value you first get the object with const value = await database.get(path) and then create a proxy with the path proxy(value, path) . There a couple different things you can subscribe to, the first way to subscribe is to any changes on the database with subscribe(obj, callback) the second way is to if a certain key on an object changes(this key can’t be another object; it should be a primitive: subscribe(obj, callback, key) the third way is subscribing to if a key is added or removed to an object with subscribeKeys(obj, callback) .

docs.text:
Preact ¶ See the preact docs for more info (note the preact docs use jsx in most places its quite similar to htm but uses {} instead of ${}) HTM and Preact are Javascript libraries that help with creating UI components. To be able to have reusable components (switches, sliders, etc.) we want some way to create a function that can contain all the logic and markup to render this component. The way we do this is with preact and htm. We use htm to create the markup, it useless a tagged template literal a template literal is just a javascript string that can include interpolations (javascript expressions within them) for example for an untagged template: const string = `
hello ${ 1 }
` string == "
hello 1
" the tagged template literal from htm allows us to write regular html as well as including javascript within it using the interpolations ( ${} ). The element’s children and attributes that you write get passed to preact for rendering. Preact then uses that structure to create a virtual dom (vdom), a tree of the nodes and their attributes and children similar to the dom in the browser. This vdom can then be rendered within a parent element with the render function. To create a component you create a normal javascript function that takes an object with the attributes that can be set on it (sometimes called properties, or props). This component can be included in any html template like so: html ` < ${ Component } attribute1="test">

child element

Where Component is defined as: function Component ({ attribute1 , children }) { return html `
${ children }
` } Components functions can have other logic and such within them, and when any of their attributes/props change they cause a re-render. This re-render only changes the vdom, not the actual webpage. Then preact will figure out what changed and then make those changes to the page https://preactjs.com/guide/v10/getting-started and the tutorial for more info Preact Signals ¶ https://preactjs.com/guide/v10/signals signal.value and effect ¶ In concept a signal is just an object with a value key that signals some ui or functions when that value changes. To create a signal you use const num = signal(1) and then access the value with num.value Then one way to have a function that responds to this signal is with the effect function. The effect function runs the callback given to it and tracks which signals were accessed; when those signals values have changed, it reruns the function, the next function is computed which takes a callback to compute a new value from one or more signals and returns a readonly signal whose value will be recomputed whenever the signals it depends on changes. Reactive UI ¶ Just like how props changing can cause preact to rerender, if signals are used in an html template it will cause preact to rerender. Signals also create another way to do a “fine grained” update to the ui if you pass the signal object instead of the object.value preact won’t cause a rerender(calling the component function again to regenerate the virtual dom to be diffed). Some of our components take signals as attributes this allows to easily integrate with our wrapped signals for having sliders or toggles be automatically integrated with the database Hooks and wrapped signals ¶ To use signals with preact, we have to use hooks. This is because we want to use the same signal on rerenders, when creating a signal from a component we use the useSignal function which returns the same signal on each render(under the hood it uses the useMemo hook). One common way to use signals is to wrap them to add more functionality to them; this allows for integration with the database and state proxy.
docs.title: Preact

docs.text: Getting Started With Aether ¶ Standalone Server - Raspberry Pi ¶ Initial Setup ¶ A standalone Server can be quickly created using a pre-made Raspberry Pi image. (Contact PBSI for access to a pre built image) This Image can be flashed to a 16GB SD card using the Raspberry Pi imager. The image has been tested with a Raspberry Pi 5 (4GB RAM) and a Raspberry Pi 4 (4GB of RAM). The Pi is configured with a desktop environment but should be able to be used primarily remotely without a display, mouse or keyboard. After starting up the PI a new WiFi network will appear called "Aether-Local". Connecting to this network will give you access to Aether. If the Pi is also connected to the internet via Ethernet then the wireless Aether-Local network will also provide internet access. Once a device is connected to the Aether-Local network it can access the Aether interface by opening a web browser (preferably Chrome) and going to: http://aether:3022/ this should open the Aether Login page: An administrator user will be configured for you and credentials provided with the pre-built image. Once logged in you can navigate to the Dashboard Panel to see connected Node Controllers. Adding Node Controllers. ¶ Aether's Node Controllers are the distributed devices co-ordinated by the Aether interface. Custom Node Controller PCB's developed by PBSI can be used, or an Adafruit ESP32 Huzzah Feather can be configured as a Node Controller. Troubleshooting the Standalone Server: ¶ On the Aether-Local network you can access a terminal on the PI via SSH. Open a command prompt and enter ssh aether@aether the password for SSH to the Pi will be provided with the Image. To access the Aether Server logs use the command pm2 logs server To stop and start the server use: pm2 stop server and pm2 start server if the server was working well but has crashed and won't restart its possible that the database has been corrupted. You can restore to the last functional state by navigating to the server directory with: cd testbed-v4 and running: git restore . Troubleshooting Devices: ¶ Right clicking on a device in the dashboard will show you its IP address. With Its IP address you can connect to the device from a command prompt or terminal via telnet: telnet [DEVICE IP] This will give you a print out of the devices debug outputs. There are 3 levels of debug information that can be cycled through by entering "d" into the telnet terminal. Custom Server ¶ Github ¶ Ask for access to https://github.com/pbarch/testbed-v4 (from TODO) and clone it with git clone https://github.com/pbarch/testbed-v4 Node.js & NPM ¶ Nodejs is used for running the server and firebase database On windows or macos Install nodejs and npm from https://nodejs.org/en/download/prebuilt-installer if needed. Once node and npm are installed install dependencies with: cd Testbed_Server npm install Zig ¶ Zig is used for building the wasm modules and to write build scripts Download zig version 0.13.0 from https://ziglang.org/download/ and unzip it to testbed-v4/NCv4_Firmware/zig , the zig binary will be located at zig/zig Platformio ¶ The arduino firmware can be built with platformio cli this can be installed by running python get-platformio.py Firmware can be uploaded over usb with pio run -t upload -e [ENV] where [ENV] is the env from platformio.ino for the plugged in board There are zig build scripts to facilitate the OTA workflow and can be used with zig build arduino
docs.title: Getting Started With Aether

docs.text: Logic Reactor ¶ The Logic Reactor defines the internal logic of a Node Controller, and provides a graph based interface to configure logical operations and behaviours. Graph Settings ¶ The Graph Settings panel shows the currently selected graph, and Node Controller. Block Parameters ¶ The Block Parameters panel shows the parameters that can be adjusted on the selected block. Individual block parameters are described in detail in the Blocks Section below, but some features are common to all blocks: Surfaced: The Surfaced toggle determines whether or not this blocks parameters are made visible in the dashboard. A Node Controller's surfaced parameters appear in the Hardware panel when it is selected in the dashboard. Blocks ¶ Software Inputs ¶ Software Input blocks create continuous inputs to the logic graph. These inputs can be manipulate via the Logic Editor user interface, or Surfaced to be made visible in the Hardware Panel of the Dashboard. Button: ¶ The button block provides a momentary input of 1.0 when pressed in the Logic Editor. When not pressed the button provides an input of 0.0. Slider: ¶ The slider provides a continuous input between 0.0 and 1.0 depending on how it is set. Grab the handle to adjust the value in the Logic Editor. Switch: ¶ The switch provides a continuous input of 0.0 or 1.0 depending on its state. Click the switch in the Logic Editor to toggle its state. Integer: ¶ The integer input provides a continuous integer value. Use the plus or minus buttons in the Logic Editor to adjust its value. External Inputs ¶ Hardware Inputs are driven from the Analog & Digital sensor readings on a Node Controller. When a Node Controller is selected in the Touch: ¶ The Touch block provides input from the Capacitive Touch sensor of the Node controller. The touch block also provides parameter to remap the raw touch values from the Node controller to a 0.0 to 1.0 range: Name Description rest The Baseline touch value, use the "Set Rest" button to set this value when nothing is touching the capacitive touch sensor hard The Maximum touch value, use the "Set Hard" button to set this value while firmly gripping the capacitive touch sensor interpolation Not yet used Analog Sensor: ¶ The Analog Sensor block provides input from the Analog Sensor attached to the Node Controller. This block also provides parameters to remap the sensor range: Name Description floor The Baseline value, used for remapping ceiling The Maximum touch value, used for remapping Influence: ¶ The Influence Input provides the current influence of the selected Influence Engine on this Node Controller ( Modulated by the Node Controllers React Ability's Subscription level ) Message Input: ¶ The message input allows the Node controller to listen for input over OSC. The key parameter allows you to define the OSC address this block should listen to. Messages with the address format /logic/[key] will be received by this block. Operators ¶ Trigger Threshold: ¶ Trigger threshold sets a threshold value that must be reached before a value is emitted. Note the trigger value is equal to the input value, as long as the input value is above the trigger Threshold. The Tigger Threshold block has the following parameters: Name Description trigThreshold Threshold value above which this block outputs a trigger value trigReset Value below which the latch resets flagTrigLatch If enabled, the input value must drop below the trigRest value before this block will output another trigger value. trigCooldownMs Defines a cooldown time between the output of trigger values. Sequence: ¶ The Sequence operator plays back a user defined sequence when it receives an Input. Use the Edit Sequence in the block parameters to draw a new sequence. Propagation: ¶ A delay. The propegation operator starts a timeline when it receives an input, at the specified point on the timeline it outputs an attenuated trigger value. Math: ¶ The math operator allows you to select a math operation to be done on it's two input values. The available operations are: Symbol Desciption + Addition - Subtraction X Multiplication ÷ Division Note: Division by 0 will return Infinity % Modulo > Greater Than Note: Returns 1.0 if True, 0.0 if False < Less Than Note: Returns 1.0 if True, 0.0 if False == Is Equal To Note: Returns 1.0 if True, 0.0 if False != Is Not Equal To Note: Returns 1.0 if True, 0.0 if False MAX Maximum. docs.title: Logic Reactor

docs.text: The Logic Reactor defines the internal logic of a Node Controller, and provides a graph based interface to configure logical operations and behaviours.
docs.title: Logic Reactor

docs.text: The Graph Settings panel shows the currently selected graph, and Node Controller.
docs.title: Graph Settings

docs.text: The Block Parameters panel shows the parameters that can be adjusted on the selected block. Individual block parameters are described in detail in the Blocks Section below, but some features are common to all blocks: Surfaced: The Surfaced toggle determines whether or not this blocks parameters are made visible in the dashboard. A Node Controller's surfaced parameters appear in the Hardware panel when it is selected in the dashboard.
docs.title: Block Parameters

docs.text: Software Input blocks create continuous inputs to the logic graph. These inputs can be manipulate via the Logic Editor user interface, or Surfaced to be made visible in the Hardware Panel of the Dashboard.
docs.title: Software Inputs

docs.text: The button block provides a momentary input of 1.0 when pressed in the Logic Editor. When not pressed the button provides an input of 0.0.
docs.title: Button:

docs.text: The slider provides a continuous input between 0.0 and 1.0 depending on how it is set. Grab the handle to adjust the value in the Logic Editor.
docs.title: Slider:

docs.text: The switch provides a continuous input of 0.0 or 1.0 depending on its state. Click the switch in the Logic Editor to toggle its state.
docs.title: Switch:

docs.text: The integer input provides a continuous integer value. Use the plus or minus buttons in the Logic Editor to adjust its value.
docs.title: Integer:

docs.text: Hardware Inputs are driven from the Analog & Digital sensor readings on a Node Controller. When a Node Controller is selected in the
docs.title: External Inputs

docs.text: The Touch block provides input from the Capacitive Touch sensor of the Node controller. The touch block also provides parameter to remap the raw touch values from the Node controller to a 0.0 to 1.0 range: Name Description rest The Baseline touch value, use the "Set Rest" button to set this value when nothing is touching the capacitive touch sensor hard The Maximum touch value, use the "Set Hard" button to set this value while firmly gripping the capacitive touch sensor interpolation Not yet used
docs.title: Touch:

docs.text: The Analog Sensor block provides input from the Analog Sensor attached to the Node Controller. This block also provides parameters to remap the sensor range: Name Description floor The Baseline value, used for remapping ceiling The Maximum touch value, used for remapping
docs.title: Analog Sensor:

docs.text: The Influence Input provides the current influence of the selected Influence Engine on this Node Controller ( Modulated by the Node Controllers React Ability's Subscription level )
docs.title: Influence:

docs.text: The message input allows the Node controller to listen for input over OSC. The key parameter allows you to define the OSC address this block should listen to. Messages with the address format /logic/[key] will be received by this block.
docs.title: Message Input:

docs.text: Trigger threshold sets a threshold value that must be reached before a value is emitted. Note the trigger value is equal to the input value, as long as the input value is above the trigger Threshold. The Tigger Threshold block has the following parameters: Name Description trigThreshold Threshold value above which this block outputs a trigger value trigReset Value below which the latch resets flagTrigLatch If enabled, the input value must drop below the trigRest value before this block will output another trigger value. trigCooldownMs Defines a cooldown time between the output of trigger values.
docs.title: Trigger Threshold:

docs.text: The Sequence operator plays back a user defined sequence when it receives an Input. Use the Edit Sequence in the block parameters to draw a new sequence.
docs.title: Sequence:

docs.text: A delay. The propegation operator starts a timeline when it receives an input, at the specified point on the timeline it outputs an attenuated trigger value.
docs.title: Propagation:

docs.text: The math operator allows you to select a math operation to be done on it's two input values. The available operations are: Symbol Desciption + Addition - Subtraction X Multiplication ÷ Division Note: Division by 0 will return Infinity % Modulo > Greater Than Note: Returns 1.0 if True, 0.0 if False < Less Than Note: Returns 1.0 if True, 0.0 if False == Is Equal To Note: Returns 1.0 if True, 0.0 if False != Is Not Equal To Note: Returns 1.0 if True, 0.0 if False MAX Maximum. Returns the greater of the two values MIN Minimum. Returns the lesser of the two values
